# 🤖 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence — Index

> [!abstract] Course Overview
> A structured vault for AI fundamentals: agent design, rational behaviour, search algorithms, and problem-solving strategies. Based on **Russell & Norvig** — *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*.

### 📌 Uninformed Search
- [ ] BFS — data structure, all 4 properties, time $O(b^d)$, space $O(b^d)$
- [ ] DFS — data structure, all 4 properties, why **NOT** complete in infinite spaces
- [ ] UCS — priority queue ordered by $g(n)$, when does it reduce to BFS?

### 📌 Informed Search
- [ ] Define $h(n)$ and properties it must satisfy
- [ ] Define **admissible** heuristic: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ — never overestimates
- [ ] Define **consistent** heuristic: triangle inequality; state consistent ⟹ admissible
- [ ] Greedy Best-First: $f(n) = h(n)$, NOT complete, NOT optimal
- [ ] **A* formula**: $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ — must know perfectly
- [ ] A* optimality: admissible $h$ for tree search; consistent $h$ for graph search
- [ ] Hill climbing — 3 failure modes: **local maxima**, **plateaus**, **ridges**
- [ ] Heuristic dominance: $h_1(n) \geq h_2(n)$ for all $n$ ⟹ prefer $h_1$

# 🎯 Informed (Heuristic) Search

> [!abstract] Topic Overview
> Informed search uses **problem-specific knowledge** — a heuristic function $h(n)$ — to guide search more efficiently than blind/uninformed methods. Covers Greedy Best-First Search, A* Search, Hill Climbing, and Simulated Annealing.

---

## What is Informed Search?

- Also called **Heuristic Search**
- Uses a **heuristic function** $h(n)$ to estimate the cost remaining to the goal
- Guides search toward ==promising states== rather than blindly expanding all nodes
- Can dramatically reduce the number of nodes explored compared to uninformed search

---

## Heuristic Function $h(n)$

- **Definition**: Estimated cost from node $n$ to the ==nearest goal== state
- Must satisfy: $h(goal) = 0$ — cost at the goal is zero
- Must be: $h(n) \geq 0$ — non-negative for all nodes
- Is **problem-specific** — a different $h$ is designed for each domain

### Admissible Heuristic

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n)$$

- $h^*(n)$ = **true/actual** cost from $n$ to the nearest goal
- ==Never overestimates== the actual cost to reach the goal
- Required for A* to be **optimal in tree search**
- Example: straight-line distance is admissible for road navigation (real roads are always longer)

### Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic

$$h(n) \leq c(n,\ a,\ n') + h(n')$$

- **Triangle inequality**: estimated cost from $n$ ≤ step cost to successor $n'$ + estimated cost from $n'$
- ==Consistency implies admissibility== (but admissibility does NOT imply consistency)
- Required for A* to be **optimal in graph search** (with repeated state checking)

> [!tip] Relationship to Remember
> Consistent ⟹ Admissible (one-way implication only)
> Admissible ↛ Consistent

---

## Common Heuristic Examples

| Heuristic | Formula | Use Case |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Manhattan Distance** | $h(n) = \|x_1 - x_2\| +

tionship to Remember
> Consistent ⟹ Admissible (one-way implication only)
> Admissible ↛ Consistent

---

## Common Heuristic Examples

| Heuristic | Formula | Use Case |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Manhattan Distance** | $h(n) = \|x_1 - x_2\| + \|y_1 - y_2\|$ | Grid/tile puzzles (only horizontal + vertical moves) |
| **Euclidean Distance** | $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_1-x_2)^2 + (y_1-y_2)^2}$ | Continuous 2D/3D space |
| **Misplaced Tiles** $h_1$ | Count of tiles not in their goal position | 8-puzzle (weaker) |
| **Manhattan for 8-puzzle** $h_2$ | Sum of Manhattan distances of all tiles from goal | 8-puzzle (stronger) |
| **Straight-Line Distance** | Direct Euclidean distance to goal city | Romania map navigation |

> [!example] 8-Puzzle: Comparing Heuristics
> State: `[7,2,4 / 5,0,6 / 8,3,1]` → Goal: `[1,2,3 / 4,5,6 / 7,8,0]`
> - $h_1$ (misplaced tiles) = **7** tiles out of place
> - $h_2$ (Manhattan distance) = sum of all individual tile distances = **18**
> - $h_2$ dominates $h_1$ — both admissible but $h_2$ gives tighter estimate → expands fewer nodes

---

## 1. Greedy Best-First Search

> **Evaluation function**: $f(n) = h(n)$

- Expands the node that ==appears closest to the goal== based on the heuristic alone
- Ignores the actual path cost $g(n)$ entirely — "greedy" about reaching the goal quickly
- Uses a priority queue ordered by $h(n)$

| Property | Result | Explanation |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Complete** | ==No== | Can get stuck in loops or dead ends — no path cost tracking |
| **Optimal** | ==No== | Ignores actual cost; may find a suboptimal or very long path |
| **Time** | $O(b^m)$ worst case | With a good heuristic, much better in practice |
| **Space** | $O(b^m)$ | Keeps all generated nodes in memory |

> [!warning] Greedy is Neither Complete nor Optimal
> Greedy search can take a very long detour or cycle forever. It is **fast but unreliable** — only use when you need a quick approximation, not a guaranteed optimal solution.

---

## 2. A* Search ⭐ — The Gold Standard

>

dy is Neither Complete nor Optimal
> Greedy search can take a very long detour or cycle forever. It is **fast but unreliable** — only use when you need a quick approximation, not a guaranteed optimal solution.

---

## 2. A* Search ⭐ — The Gold Standard

> **Evaluation function**: ==$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$==

> [!important] The Most Important AI Search Algorithm
> A* combines actual cost and estimated remaining cost for the **best of both worlds**. Know this formula perfectly — it is the single most tested formula in AI.

$$\boxed{f(n) = g(n) + h(n)}$$

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Actual cost from start to $n$ | $g(n)$ | How far you have **gone** |
| Estimated cost from $n$ to goal | $h(n)$ | How far you **hope** to go |
| Estimated total path cost through $n$ | $f(n)$ | Total estimated journey |

**Strategy**: Expand the node with the ==lowest $f(n)$== value (min-priority queue on $f(n)$)

| Property | Result | Explanation |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Complete** | ==Yes== | Will find a solution if one exists and step costs $> 0$ |
| **Optimal** | ==Yes== | With **admissible** $h$ (tree search) or **consistent** $h$ (graph search) |
| **Time** | $O(b^d)$ | Exponential but well-guided — far fewer nodes than BFS in practice |
| **Space** | $O(b^d)$ | Keeps all nodes in memory — ==main practical weakness== |

> [!tip] Memory Aid for A*
> - **g** = "**G**one" distance (actual cost already paid)
> - **h** = "**H**opeful" distance (optimistic estimate of remaining cost)
> - **f** = Gone + Hopeful = full estimated journey cost

---

## 3. Hill Climbing

> **Strategy**: Always move to the neighbour with the **best evaluation value** (local search — no path tracking)

- Keeps only the **current state** — no frontier, no history
- Not a path-finding algorithm — finds a **local optimum** in the state space
- Like climbing a foggy mountain: can only see immediate neighbours, not the summit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
 A[Start: Random / Initial State] --> B{Better neighbour exists?}
 B -->|Yes| C[Move to best neighbour]
 C --> B
 B -->|No| D[Return current state as solution]
 style D fill:#8b4513,color:#fff
 style A fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
```

### Three Failure Modes

> [!warning] Hill Climbing Failure Modes — Know All Three

**1. 🏔️ Local Maxima**
- Agent is at a state better than all immediate neighbours, but **NOT** the global maximum
- Hill climbing stops here — has no mechanism to move downhill to escape
- The most common failure mode

**2. 🏜️ Plateaus**
- Flat region where all neighbours have the **same evaluation value**
- No gradient to follow — agent cannot determine a direction
- Variant: random sideways moves can help escape flat plateaus

**3. ⛰️ Ridges**
- Narrow elevated area oriented **diagonally** relative to available moves
- Agent oscillates without making progress — moves are either all downhill or flat
- Requires lookahead or different move operators to traverse

### Hill Climbing Variants

| Variant | Strategy | Advantage |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Steepest-Ascent** | Move to the **best**

ng progress — moves are either all downhill or flat
- Requires lookahead or different move operators to traverse

### Hill Climbing Variants

| Variant | Strategy | Advantage |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| **Steepest-Ascent** | Move to the **best** of all neighbours | Maximally greedy |
| **Stochastic** | Random choice **among uphill** moves | Avoids narrow ridges |
| **First-Choice** | Move to the **first** neighbour that is better | Fast for large branching factor |
| **Random Restart** | Restart from random positions, keep best result | ==Surprisingly effective!== |

> [!tip] Random Restart Hill Climbing
> With $k$ random restarts, the probability of **not** finding the global maximum is $(1-p)^k$ where $p$ = probability of success per restart. As $k$ increases, success approaches certainty. Simple yet effective for many practical problems.

---

## 4. Simulated Annealing

- Inspired by the **physical annealing** process in metallurgy (slowly cooling heated metal to reduce defects)
- Allows ==bad moves to be accepted with decreasing probability== — can escape local maxima
- As **temperature $T$ decreases**, the algorithm transitions from random walk → hill climbing

**Probability of accepting a worse state**:

$$P(\text{accept worse state}) = e^{\Delta E / T}$$

Where $\Delta E$ = quality change (negative for a worse state), $T$ = current temperature

> [!tip] Simulated Annealing Intuition
> - **High temperature** → accepts almost any move (exploration / random walk)
> - **Low temperature** → accepts only better moves (exploitation / hill climbing)
> - **Slow cooling schedule** → converges to a near-optimal solution with high probability

---

## Greedy Best-First vs A* Comparison

> [!important] Key Exam Comparison Table

| Property | Greedy Best-First | A* Search |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Evaluation function** | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| **Complete** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Optimal** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (admissible $h$) |
| **Considers actual path cost** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes

rst | A* Search |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Evaluation function** | $f(n) = h(n)$ | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ |
| **Complete** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Optimal** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (admissible $h$) |
| **Considers actual path cost** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Speed** | ⚡ Fast (fewer expansions) | Slower but guaranteed |
| **Memory** | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **Use case** | Quick approximation | Guaranteed optimal solution |

---

## Heuristic Dominance

$$h_1 \text{ dominates } h_2 \iff h_1(n) \geq h_2(n) \text{ for all nodes } n$$

- If $h_1$ dominates $h_2$, then **$h_1$ is the better (more informed) heuristic**
- A dominant heuristic expands ==fewer nodes== — provides tighter lower bounds
- The ideal heuristic is $h^*(n)$ (exact cost) — but computing it is usually as hard as the original problem
- Choose the **most dominant admissible heuristic** available

> [!example] 8-Puzzle Dominance
> - $h_2$ (Manhattan distance) $\geq$ $h_1$ (misplaced tiles) for all states
> - Therefore $h_2$ **dominates** $h_1$ → A* with $h_2$ expands significantly fewer nodes
> - Always prefer $h_2$ when both are available

---

## Informed vs Uninformed Search Summary

| Aspect | Uninformed (BFS/IDS) | Informed (A*) |
|--------|---------------------|--------------|
| Uses heuristic $h(n)$ | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Guaranteed optimal | ✅ Yes (IDS with equal costs) | ✅ Yes (admissible $h$) |
| Nodes expanded | Many — blind exploration | Few — guided by $h(n)$ |
| Domain knowledge needed | None | Yes — must design $h(n)$ |
| Best algorithm | IDS | A* |

## 🧠 Flashcards
#flashcard

A* search evaluation function :: f(n) = g(n) + h(n). g = actual cost from start to n ("Gone"), h = estimated cost from n to goal ("Hopeful"). A* expands the node with smallest f.

Greedy Best-First Search evaluation function :: f(n) = h(n) — only the heuristic estimate; ignores actual path cost g(n). Fast but can pick a bad path because it never accounts for cost already paid.

Admissible heuristic definition :: h(n) ≤ h*(n) for every node n — never overestimates the true remaining cost. Example: straight-line distance is admissible for road navigation because real roads can only be longer or equal.

Consistent (monotone) heuristic :: For every edge (n → n') of cost c: h(n) ≤ c + h(n'). The heuristic obeys the triangle inequality. Example: straight-line distance is consistent because two legs can never beat the direct line.

Is A* complete? :: Yes, if branching factor is finite and every step cost > 0.

Is A* optimal? :: Yes — for **tree search** when h is admissible; for **graph search** with repeated-state checking, h must be consistent.

Is Greedy Best-First optimal? :: No. Example: a heuristic might point straight at the goal across a wall while the actual optimal path detours around — Greedy commits to the wrong branch because it ignores g(n).

Manhattan distance heuristic :: h(n) = |x₁−x₂| + |y₁−y₂|. Use for grid problems where moves are horizontal/vertical

raight at the goal across a wall while the actual optimal path detours around — Greedy commits to the wrong branch because it ignores g(n).

Manhattan distance heuristic :: h(n) = |x₁−x₂| + |y₁−y₂|. Use for grid problems where moves are horizontal/vertical only (e.g., 8-puzzle, Pac-Man grids).

Misplaced tiles heuristic (8-puzzle) :: Count of tiles not in their goal position. Admissible but weaker than Manhattan, which sums each tile's individual distance.

Why is consistent ⟹ admissible but not vice-versa? :: A consistent heuristic produces non-decreasing f-values along any path, which guarantees the first time A* expands a node, it has found the optimal path to it (no re-expansion needed). Admissibility alone allows re-expansions and is a strictly weaker condition.

Heuristic dominance :: h₁ dominates h₂ if h₁(n) ≥ h₂(n) for all n (with both still admissible). The dominant heuristic is "more informed" and expands fewer nodes. Example: Manhattan dominates misplaced-tiles for 8-puzzle.

Hill climbing 3 failure modes
?
1. **Local maximum** — a peak surrounded by lower neighbours that is not the global peak; hill climbing stops here because no move improves.
2. **Plateau** — flat region where all neighbours have equal value; no gradient, agent has nowhere to go.
3. **Ridge** — a peak that runs diagonal to the available moves; each axis-aligned step is downhill so the agent oscillates without progress.

How does Random Restart Hill Climbing escape local maxima? :: Restart from a fresh random state whenever stuck, keep the best result across restarts. With k restarts, P(missing global) = (1−p)^k → 0. Surprisingly competitive on real problems.

Simulated annealing acceptance probability :: P(accept worse move) = e^(ΔE / T), where ΔE < 0 for a worse move and T is the current temperature. As T → 0 the algorithm reduces to hill climbing.

A* vs UCS in one sentence :: UCS is A* with h(n) = 0; the heuristic is what lets A* skip exploring nodes that look unpromising.

**Answer: B** — Admissibility guarantees A* tree-search optimality.

**Answer: B** — Required for A* graph-search optimality.

# 🤖 Introduction to Artificial Intelligence

> [!abstract] Topic Overview
> Covers the definition and scope of AI, the Turing Test, rational agents, the PEAS framework, environment classification, and the five types of agents. Foundation for all further AI study.

---

## What is Artificial Intelligence?

Four perspectives on AI (Russell & Norvig) — arranged in a 2×2 grid:

| | **Human-based** | **Ideal / Rational** |
|---|---|---|
| **Thinking** | Thinking Humanly *(cognitive modelling)* | Thinking Rationally *(laws of thought)* |
| **Acting** | Acting Humanly *(Turing Test)* | ==Acting Rationally *(rational agents)*== |

> [!note] Modern AI Focus
> Modern AI focuses on ==Acting Rationally== — building agents that do the **right thing**, not necessarily the *human* thing. Rational behaviour is measurable; human-like thinking is not well-defined.

---

## Turing Test (1950, Alan Turing)

- **Original name**: "Imitation Game" — proposed in the paper *"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"*
- A machine **passes** if a human interrogator **cannot distinguish** it from a human after a text-based conversation
- **Requirements** for a machine to pass the standard Turing Test:
 - ==Natural Language Processing (NLP)== — communicate in human language
 - ==Knowledge Representation== — store and retrieve what it knows
 - ==Automated Reasoning== — use stored info to answer questions and draw conclusions
 - ==Machine Learning== — adapt to new circumstances, detect patterns

> [!info] Total Turing Test
> Adds a **video signal** — requires **Computer Vision** (to perceive objects) and **Robotics** (to physically interact with the world). Tests the full spectrum of human-level intelligence.

> [!warning] Turing Test Criticism
> The Turing Test is not a reliable benchmark for intelligence. A system can **pass without being intelligent** (e.g., ELIZA,

(to physically interact with the world). Tests the full spectrum of human-level intelligence.

> [!warning] Turing Test Criticism
> The Turing Test is not a reliable benchmark for intelligence. A system can **pass without being intelligent** (e.g., ELIZA, 1966) or **be intelligent without passing** (chess computers). Modern AI does not aim to pass it.

---

## Rational Agents

- **Agent**: anything that ==perceives its environment== through **sensors** and ==acts upon it== through **actuators**
- **Percept**: the current input the agent receives from its sensors
- **Percept sequence**: the complete history of all percepts received
- **Rational agent**: selects actions that ==maximise expected performance measure==, given its percept sequence and built-in knowledge
- Rationality ≠ omniscience — a rational agent does the best it can with available info

```mermaid
graph LR
 E[Environment] -->|Percept| S[Sensors]
 S --> D[Agent Program / Decision Making]
 D --> A[Actuators]
 A -->|Action| E
 style D fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
 style E fill:#2d4a2d,color:#fff
```

---

## PEAS Framework

> [!important] PEAS is Frequently Tested
> Be able to define PEAS for **any given agent** scenario. PEAS = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors.

| Letter | Component | Meaning |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **P** | Performance Measure | How *success* is evaluated — what we want the agent to maximise |
| **E** | Environment | Where the agent *operates* — the external world it interacts with |
| **A** | Actuators | How the agent *acts* on the environment — output mechanisms |
| **S** | Sensors | How the agent *perceives* the world — input mechanisms |

### PEAS Examples Table

| Agent | Performance Measure | Environment | Actuators | Sensors |
|-------|-------------------|-------------|-----------|---------|
| 🚕 **Taxi Driver** | Safe, fast, legal, comfortable, profit | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, other vehicles | Steering, brake, accelerator, signal, horn | Cameras, GPS, speedometer, sonar, engine sensors |
|

--|-------------|-----------|---------|
| 🚕 **Taxi Driver** | Safe, fast, legal, comfortable, profit | Roads, traffic, pedestrians, weather, other vehicles | Steering, brake, accelerator, signal, horn | Cameras, GPS, speedometer, sonar, engine sensors |
| 🏥 **Medical Diagnosis** | Correct diagnosis, low cost, patient wellbeing | Patient, hospital, lab equipment, staff | Display, test orders, prescriptions, referrals | Keyboard, patient records, lab results |
| 🤖 **Vacuum Cleaner** | Clean floor, efficiency, battery conservation | Room, floor types, dirt, furniture | Wheels, vacuum/suction motor, brush | Dirt sensor, location sensor, bump sensor, cliff sensor |
| ♟️ **Chess Player** | Win game, legal moves, minimal time | Chessboard, opponent, clock | Chess piece movements | Board state sensor (complete) |

---

## Environment Types

> [!important] Know All 6 Properties — Exam Essential
> You must be able to classify **any** given environment across all 6 dimensions. This appears on virtually every AI exam.

| Property | Options | Key Question |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Observable** | Fully / Partially | Can the agent see the **complete** state at each point? |
| **Deterministic** | Deterministic / Stochastic | Is the next state **fully determined** by current state + action? |
| **Episodic** | Episodic / Sequential | Is each episode **independent**, or do current actions affect the future? |
| **Static** | Static / Dynamic | Does the environment **change** while the agent is deliberating? |
| **Discrete** | Discrete / Continuous | Is there a **finite** number of percepts/actions, or infinite? |
| **Agents** | Single / Multi-agent | Are there **one or multiple** agents operating simultaneously? |

> [!tip] Mnemonic: "ODESSA"
> **O**bservable · **D**eterministic · **E**pisodic · **S**tatic · **S**ingle/Multi · discre**t**e (**A**close)
> Not a perfect acronym but enough to recall all 6 dimensions under pressure.

> [!warning] Common Confusion
> - **Deterministic** = predictability of outcome

le · **D**eterministic · **E**pisodic · **S**tatic · **S**ingle/Multi · discre**t**e (**A**close)
> Not a perfect acronym but enough to recall all 6 dimensions under pressure.

> [!warning] Common Confusion
> - **Deterministic** = predictability of outcome (same state + action → same result every time)
> - **Static** = environment does NOT change *while the agent is choosing its action*
> These are independent properties — an environment can be deterministic but dynamic!

> [!example] Chess Environment Classification
> ✅ **Fully Observable** (both players see the whole board) · ✅ **Deterministic** (moves have exact outcomes) · **Sequential** (each move affects future) · **Static** (board does not change between turns) · **Discrete** (finite positions) · **Multi-agent** (two players)

> [!example] Taxi Driving Environment Classification
> ⚠️ **Partially Observable** (sensors have limits) · ⚠️ **Stochastic** (other drivers unpredictable) · **Sequential** (every decision affects future) · **Dynamic** (traffic changes during deliberation) · **Continuous** (infinite positions/speeds) · **Multi-agent** (many vehicles and pedestrians)

---

## Agent Types (Simple → Complex)

### 1. 🟡 Simple Reflex Agent
- Acts based **only on current percept** — uses condition-action (if-then) rules
- **No memory**, no model of the world
- Fails completely in **partially observable** environments
- Example: Thermostat ("if temp < 20°C → heat on"), basic vacuum cleaner

### 2. 🟠 Model-Based Reflex Agent
- Maintains an **internal state** — a model of the world that it cannot directly observe
- Updates model using: current percept + transition model (how world evolves) + action model (what actions do)
- Handles **partial observability** better than simple reflex
- Example: Car with internal map, robot tracking its previous positions

### 3. 🔵 Goal-Based Agent
- Has **explicit goals** — desired states to achieve
- Uses **planning and search** to find sequences of actions that reach the goal
- More flexible than reflex agents — can

th internal map, robot tracking its previous positions

### 3. 🔵 Goal-Based Agent
- Has **explicit goals** — desired states to achieve
- Uses **planning and search** to find sequences of actions that reach the goal
- More flexible than reflex agents — can change behaviour when goal changes
- Example: Route-planning robot, puzzle-solving agent

### 4. 🟣 Utility-Based Agent
- Has a **utility function** — maps states to real numbers (measure of "happiness")
- Selects actions that **maximise expected utility**
- Handles **uncertainty** and **trade-offs** between competing goals
- Example: Medical diagnosis system balancing accuracy vs patient comfort vs cost

### 5. 🟢 Learning Agent
- Can **improve performance** through experience — starts with limited knowledge
- **Four components**:
 - ==Learning element==: makes improvements to the agent based on feedback
 - ==Performance element==: selects external actions (the "rest" of the agent)
 - ==Critic==: evaluates performance against a fixed performance standard
 - ==Problem generator==: suggests exploratory actions to gain new experience

```mermaid
graph TD
 A[Learning Agent] --> B[Learning Element]
 A --> C[Performance Element]
 A --> D[Critic]
 A --> E[Problem Generator]
 B -->|"Improvements"| C
 D -->|"Feedback / Evaluation"| B
 E -->|"Exploration suggestions"| C
 F[Environment] -->|"Percepts"| C
 F -->|"Percepts"| D
 C -->|"Actions"| F
 style A fill:#2d5a27,color:#fff
 style B fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
```

```mermaid
graph TD
 A[Simple Reflex] -->|add internal state| B[Model-Based Reflex]
 B -->|add goals| C[Goal-Based]
 C -->|add utility function| D[Utility-Based]
 D -->|add learning| E[Learning Agent]
 style E fill:#2d5a27,color:#fff
 style A fill:#8b4513,color:#fff
```

## ⚖️ AI Ethics

> [!important] AI Ethics is increasingly tested — understand the core concerns!

### Core Ethical Issues in AI

#### 1. Bias and Fairness
- AI systems trained on **biased data** produce biased outcomes
- Example: Facial recognition systems misidentify darker-skinned individuals more often
- Example: Hiring

nderstand the core concerns!

### Core Ethical Issues in AI

#### 1. Bias and Fairness
- AI systems trained on **biased data** produce biased outcomes
- Example: Facial recognition systems misidentify darker-skinned individuals more often
- Example: Hiring algorithms that discriminate against women if trained on male-dominated data
- **Solution**: Diverse training data, bias audits, fairness metrics

#### 2. Privacy and Surveillance
- AI enables **mass surveillance** and profiling at scale
- Facial recognition in public spaces raises privacy concerns
- Data collection for training AI often happens without meaningful consent
- **Solution**: Data minimization, consent frameworks, GDPR compliance

#### 3. Transparency and Explainability (XAI)
- Many AI models are **"black boxes"** — even experts cannot explain why a decision was made
- High-stakes decisions (medical diagnosis, loan approval, parole) require explainable reasoning
- **Solution**: Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, interpretable models

#### 4. Accountability
- **Who is responsible** when an AI system causes harm?
- Example: Self-driving car causes a fatality — is it the manufacturer, programmer, or user?
- **Solution**: Clear accountability frameworks, AI governance policies

#### 5. Autonomy and Human Control
- Risk of humans over-relying on AI decisions (automation bias)
- Concern about **fully autonomous weapons** (lethal autonomous weapons systems — LAWS)
- **Solution**: "Human in the loop" requirements for high-stakes decisions

#### 6. Job Displacement
- AI automation may eliminate many jobs, especially routine/repetitive work
- **Solution**: Reskilling programs, universal basic income discussions, managed transition

#### 7. Existential Risk (Long-term)
- Theoretical concern: superintelligent AI pursuing goals misaligned with human values
- **AI Alignment problem**: Ensuring AI systems pursue the goals we actually intend
- Proponents: Eliezer Yudkowsky, Nick Bostrom; Concern disputed by many researchers

### AI Ethics Principles (Key

ntelligent AI pursuing goals misaligned with human values
- **AI Alignment problem**: Ensuring AI systems pursue the goals we actually intend
- Proponents: Eliezer Yudkowsky, Nick Bostrom; Concern disputed by many researchers

### AI Ethics Principles (Key Frameworks)

| Principle | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| **Beneficence** | AI should benefit people and society |
| **Non-maleficence** | AI should not cause harm |
| **Autonomy** | Humans retain control and self-determination |
| **Justice** | AI benefits and risks distributed fairly |
| **Explicability** | AI decisions should be understandable |

> [!note] Real-World AI Ethics Guidelines
> - **EU AI Act** (2024): First comprehensive AI regulation; classifies AI by risk level
> - **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design**: Engineering ethics for AI systems
> - **Google AI Principles**: Beneficence, avoiding harm, safety, accountability

# 🔍 Problem Solving by Search

> [!abstract] Topic Overview
> Problem-solving agents use **search** to find action sequences that lead from an initial state to a goal state. This note covers problem formulation, state space representation, search tree structure, node data, and performance criteria for evaluating search strategies.

---

## Problem-Solving Agents

- A **problem-solving agent** is a ==goal-based agent== that decides *what to do* by finding sequences of actions leading to goal states
- Assumes the environment is: **deterministic**, **fully observable**, **discrete**, and **static**
- Key idea: **formulate** the problem → **search** for a solution → **execute** the solution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
 A[Goal Formulation] --> B[Problem Formulation]
 B --> C[Search for Solution]
 C --> D[Execute Actions]
 style A fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
 style D fill:#2d5a27,color:#fff
```

---

## Problem Formulation — 5 Components

> [!important] Memorise All 5 Components
> Standard exam question: *"List and define the 5 components of problem formulation with their notation."*

| # | Component | Definition | Notation |
|---|-----------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Initial State** | The state the agent starts in | $s_0$ |
| 2 | **Actions** | Set of actions available in state $s$ | $Actions(s)$ |
| 3 | **Transition Model** | Result of taking action $a$ in state $s$ | $Result(s,\ a) = s'$ |
| 4 | **Goal Test** | Checks whether a state is a goal state | $GoalTest(s) \rightarrow \{true,\ false\}$ |
| 5 | **Path Cost** | Total cost from initial state to node $n$ | $g(n)$ |

> [!example] 8-Puzzle Problem Formulation
> - **Initial state**: Any scrambled arrangement of 8 tiles + blank (e.g., `[7,2,4,5,0,6,8,3,1]`)
> - **Actions**: Move blank tile `Up`, `Down`, `Left`, `Right` (subject to boundary constraints)
> - **Transition model**: Resulting tile

Problem Formulation
> - **Initial state**: Any scrambled arrangement of 8 tiles + blank (e.g., `[7,2,4,5,0,6,8,3,1]`)
> - **Actions**: Move blank tile `Up`, `Down`, `Left`, `Right` (subject to boundary constraints)
> - **Transition model**: Resulting tile arrangement after moving the blank
> - **Goal test**: Tiles in order `[1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,blank]`
> - **Path cost**: Number of moves taken (each move costs 1)

> [!example] Romania Route Problem
> - **Initial state**: City = **Arad**
> - **Actions**: Drive to any directly connected adjacent city
> - **Transition model**: New current city after driving
> - **Goal test**: `current_city == Bucharest`
> - **Path cost**: Total road distance travelled (in km)

---

## State Space

- **State Space**: the set of ==all states reachable== from the initial state via any sequence of valid actions
- Represented as a **directed graph**: nodes = states, edges = actions (with costs)
- The size of the state space determines problem difficulty:

| Problem | Approximate State Space Size |
|---------|------------------------------|
| 8-Puzzle | ~362,880 states |
| 15-Puzzle | ~10 trillion states |
| Rubik's Cube | ~$4.3 \times 10^{19}$ states |
| Chess | ~$10^{40}$ legal positions |

```mermaid
graph LR
 Arad -->|140| Zerind
 Arad -->|118| Timisoara
 Arad -->|75| Sibiu
 Sibiu -->|99| Fagaras
 Fagaras -->|211| Bucharest
 Sibiu -->|80| RimnicuVilcea
 RimnicuVilcea -->|97| Pitesti
 Pitesti -->|101| Bucharest
 style Bucharest fill:#2d5a27,color:#fff
 style Arad fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
```

## Search Tree vs State Space Graph

> [!warning] Key Distinction — Frequently Examined
> A **search tree** is NOT the same as a **state space graph**. The tree can have repeated/duplicate states; the graph cannot.

| Property | Search Tree | State Space Graph |
|----------|------------|-------------------|
| Repeated states | ✅ Allowed — can revisit same state via different paths | ❌ Each state appears exactly once |
| Size | Can be **infinite** even for a finite state space | Finite if state

State Space Graph |
|----------|------------|-------------------|
| Repeated states | ✅ Allowed — can revisit same state via different paths | ❌ Each state appears exactly once |
| Size | Can be **infinite** even for a finite state space | Finite if state space is finite |
| Node meaning | Represents a **path** (history of actions from root) | Represents a **state** |
| Usage | Used directly by **search algorithms** | Represents the underlying **problem structure** |
| Memory | Stores full path history | Stores state data only |

---

## Node Data Structure

Each node in the search tree stores **5 fields**:

```
Node {
 state // the state in the state space this node corresponds to
 parent // the node in the search tree that generated this node
 action // the action that was applied to parent to generate this node
 path_cost // g(n) — total cost of the path from root to this node
 depth // number of steps from the root node
}
```

```mermaid
graph TD
 A["Arad | parent: null | action: start | g=0 | depth=0"]
 B["Sibiu | parent: Arad | action: drive | g=75 | depth=1"]
 C["Fagaras | parent: Sibiu | action: drive | g=174 | depth=2"]
 D["Bucharest | parent: Fagaras | action: drive | g=385 | depth=3"]
 A --> B
 B --> C
 C --> D
 style A fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
 style D fill:#2d5a27,color:#fff
```

## Search Strategy Performance Measures

> [!important] 4 Performance Criteria — Always Tested
> Every search algorithm is evaluated on these **4 criteria**. Be able to state all 4 for every algorithm.

| Criterion | Definition | Key Question |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| ==Completeness== | Does it **always find a solution** if one exists? | Will it ever give up or get stuck? |
| ==Optimality== | Does it find the **least-cost** solution? | Is it guaranteed to be the best path? |
| ==Time Complexity== | How many nodes are **generated** (visited)? | How fast does it scale? |
| ==Space Complexity== | How many nodes are **stored** in memory at once? | How much RAM does it need? |

## Problem Variables

> [!tip] Remember: b, d, m
> These three variables appear in **every** complexity formula. Know them cold.

$$b = \text{branching factor — maximum number of successors of any node}$$

$$d = \text{depth of the shallowest goal node in the search tree}$$

$$m = \text{maximum depth of the search tree (may be infinite)}$$

> [!example] Complexity Comparison with b=10, d=5, m=10
> - BFS time: $O(10^5) = 100{,}000$ nodes generated
> - DFS time: $O(10^{10}) = 10$ billion nodes — worst case!
> - IDS time: $O(10^5) = 100{,}000$ nodes — same as BFS with linear space

---

## Two Categories of Search

| Category | Also Called | Extra Knowledge Used? | Key Algorithms |
|----------|------------|----------------------|----------------|
| ==Uninformed== | Blind Search | ❌ No — only problem definition | BFS, DFS, UCS, DLS, IDS |
| ==Informed== | Heuristic Search | ✅ Yes — heuristic function $h(n)$ | Greedy Best-First, A*, Hill Climbing |

# 🔲 Uninformed Search Strategies

> [!abstract] Topic Overview
> Uninformed (blind) search strategies have **no additional information** beyond the problem definition — no heuristic. They differ in the **order** in which nodes are expanded from the frontier. Covers BFS, DFS, UCS, DLS, IDS, and Bidirectional Search.

---

## What is Uninformed Search?

- Also called **Blind Search** — has no heuristic function $h(n)$
- Uses only information from the **problem definition**: initial state, actions, transition model, goal test, path cost
- All strategies differ in ==which node to expand next== from the frontier (open list)
- The **frontier** is the collection of nodes generated but not yet expanded

---

## 1. Breadth-First Search (BFS)

> **Strategy**: Expand the ==shallowest== unexpanded node first

- **Data Structure**: FIFO Queue
- **Expansion Order**: Level by level — all nodes at depth $d$ before any at depth $d+1$

| Property | Result | Explanation |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Complete** | ==Yes== | Always finds a solution if one exists (assuming finite $b$) |
| **Optimal** | ==Yes*== | Finds shallowest solution — optimal only if all step costs are **equal** |
| **Time** | $O(b^d)$ | Generates all nodes at and above depth $d$ |
| **Space** | $O(b^d)$ | Stores every node in memory — ==the critical weakness== |

> [!warning] BFS Memory Explosion
> BFS must store **every generated node** in memory (the frontier AND explored set).
> - $b=10$, $d=6$: ~1.1 million nodes → ~1 GB RAM
> - $b=10$, $d=12$: ~1 trillion nodes → impossible on any modern machine

```mermaid
graph TD
 A((1 - Start)) --> B((2))
 A --> C((3))
 B --> D((4))
 B --> E((5))
 C --> F((6))
 C --> G((7 - Goal))
 style A fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
 style G fill:#2d5a27,color:#fff
```
*BFS expansion order: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 (left to right, level by

graph TD
 A((1 - Start)) --> B((2))
 A --> C((3))
 B --> D((4))
 B --> E((5))
 C --> F((6))
 C --> G((7 - Goal))

*BFS expansion order: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 (left to right, level by level)*

---

## 2. Depth-First Search (DFS)

> **Strategy**: Expand the ==deepest== unexpanded node first

- **Data Structure**: LIFO Stack (or recursion using the call stack)
- **Expansion Order**: Goes as deep as possible, backtracks when a dead-end or previously visited state is reached

| Property | Result | Explanation |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Complete** | ==No== | Can follow infinite paths and never return; Yes if state space is **finite** with loop detection |
| **Optimal** | ==No== | Finds *a* solution, not necessarily the cheapest |
| **Time** | $O(b^m)$ | Must explore the entire tree to depth $m$ in the worst case |
| **Space** | $O(bm)$ | ==Linear space!== Only stores the current path plus unexplored siblings |

> [!tip] DFS Memory Advantage
> With $b=10$, $m=100$: DFS needs only **1,000 nodes** vs BFS needing **$10^{100}$** nodes.
> DFS is the memory-efficient choice — but at the cost of completeness and optimality.

> [!warning] DFS Not Complete
> DFS is **NOT complete** in infinite state spaces or graphs with cycles, even if a solution exists at a shallow depth. Without explicit cycle detection (visited list), it will loop forever.

```mermaid
graph TD
 A((1 - Start)) --> B((2))
 A --> C((3))
 B --> D((4))
 B --> E((5 - Goal))
 C --> F((6))
 C --> G((7))
 style A fill:#4a4a8a,color:#fff
 style E fill:#2d5a27,color:#fff
```
*DFS expansion order: 1 → 2 → 4 → (backtrack) → 5 — finds goal early if lucky!*

---

## 3. Uniform Cost Search (UCS)

> **Strategy**: Expand the node with the ==lowest cumulative path cost $g(n)$==

- **Data Structure**: Min-priority queue (ordered by $g(n)$)
- Generalises BFS to handle **varying step costs** — like Dijkstra's algorithm
- Expands nodes in order of increasing $g(n)$ — pure cost, no

node with the ==lowest cumulative path cost $g(n)$==

- **Data Structure**: Min-priority queue (ordered by $g(n)$)
- Generalises BFS to handle **varying step costs** — like Dijkstra's algorithm
- Expands nodes in order of increasing $g(n)$ — pure cost, no heuristic

| Property | Result | Explanation |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Complete** | ==Yes== | If every step cost $\geq \epsilon > 0$ (no zero-cost loops) |
| **Optimal** | ==Yes== | Always finds the minimum-cost solution |
| **Time** | $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\epsilon \rfloor})$ | $C^*$ = optimal solution cost, $\epsilon$ = min step cost |
| **Space** | Same as Time | Keeps all generated nodes in memory |

> [!important] UCS = BFS Special Case
> When **all step costs are equal**, UCS reduces exactly to BFS — both expand nodes layer by layer in the same order.

---

## 4. Depth-Limited Search (DLS)

> **Strategy**: DFS with a predetermined **depth limit** $L$

- Solves the infinite-path problem of DFS by cutting off at depth $L$
- Nodes at depth $L$ are treated as if they have no successors

| Property | Result | Note |
|----------|--------|------|
| **Complete** | **No** | Fails if $d > L$ — solution missed entirely |
| **Optimal** | **No** | |
| **Time** | $O(b^L)$ | |
| **Space** | $O(bL)$ | |

> [!example] DLS Failure Case
> If the shallowest solution is at depth $d=10$ but we set $L=7$, DLS returns **failure** — even though a solution clearly exists. Choosing $L$ correctly requires domain knowledge.

---

## 5. Iterative Deepening Search (IDS)

> **Strategy**: Run DLS with increasing limits: $L = 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ \ldots$ until a solution is found

> [!important] IDS = The Preferred Uninformed Search
> IDS ==combines the best properties== of BFS and DFS:
> - ✅ **BFS**: Completeness + Optimality (with equal step costs)
> - ✅ **DFS**: Linear space efficiency
>
> Use IDS as the default uninformed search when the state space is large and depth is unknown.

| Property | Result | Explanation |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Complete** |

mality (with equal step costs)
> - ✅ **DFS**: Linear space efficiency
>
> Use IDS as the default uninformed search when the state space is large and depth is unknown.

| Property | Result | Explanation |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Complete** | ==Yes== | Finds shallowest solution — same guarantee as BFS |
| **Optimal** | ==Yes*== | With equal step costs |
| **Time** | $O(b^d)$ | Same asymptotic order as BFS |
| **Space** | $O(bd)$ | Same as DFS — only current path stored! |

**Why is repeated node expansion acceptable?**
- Nodes at depth $d$ expanded: **once**
- Nodes at depth $d-1$ expanded: $b$ extra times
- Overhead factor: $\dfrac{b}{b-1}$ — for $b=10$: only **~11% overhead** vs BFS

> [!example] IDS Overhead with b=10, d=5
> - BFS total nodes: $10^1 + 10^2 + 10^3 + 10^4 + 10^5 = 111{,}110$
> - IDS total nodes: ~$123{,}450$ (due to re-expansions)
> - **Overhead: ~11%** — negligible price for linear space!

---

## 6. Bidirectional Search

> **Strategy**: Search **forward from start** AND **backward from goal** simultaneously; stop when the two frontiers **meet in the middle**

| Property | Result |
|----------|--------|
| **Complete** | Yes |
| **Optimal** | Yes |
| **Time** | $O(b^{d/2})$ |
| **Space** | $O(b^{d/2})$ |

> [!tip] Bidirectional Time Advantage
> With $b=10$, $d=10$:
> - BFS: $O(10^{10}) = 10$ billion nodes
> - Bidirectional: $O(2 \times 10^5) = 200{,}000$ nodes — **50,000× fewer!**

**Requirements for Bidirectional Search:**
- The **goal state must be explicitly known** (not just a test)
- Actions must be **reversible** (predecessor function must exist)
- Need a way to check if the two frontiers intersect

---

## 🧠 Flashcards
#flashcard

BFS data structure :: Queue (FIFO) — First In First Out. Example: enqueue children at the back, dequeue oldest from the front, so depth-1 nodes are all expanded before any depth-2 node.

DFS data structure :: Stack (LIFO) — Last In First Out; or recursion via the call stack. Example: push children, pop the most-recently-added → algorithm dives down one branch before backtracking.

BFS time complexity :: O(b^d) where b = branching factor, d = depth of shallowest goal. Example: b=10, d=6 → ~10^6 nodes generated in the worst case.

BFS space complexity :: O(b^d) — must store every frontier node in memory; the critical weakness of BFS. Example: b=10, d=12 → ~10^12 nodes ≈ infeasible RAM.

DFS space complexity :: O(bm) where m = maximum depth — linear space; DFS's biggest advantage. Example: b=10, m=100 → only 1,000 nodes vs BFS's 10^100.

Is BFS complete? :: Yes, if branching factor b is finite. Always finds *a* solution if one exists. (Fails only when b is infinite.)

Is BFS optimal? :: Yes, **only if all step costs are equal** (it finds the shallowest goal, which is cheapest only when each edge has the same cost). With varying costs, use UCS.

Is DFS complete? :: No — in infinite state

(Fails only when b is infinite.)

Is BFS optimal? :: Yes, **only if all step costs are equal** (it finds the shallowest goal, which is cheapest only when each edge has the same cost). With varying costs, use UCS.

Is DFS complete? :: No — in infinite state spaces or in graphs with cycles (without a visited set), DFS can loop forever and never return, even when a shallow solution exists.

Is DFS optimal? :: No — DFS returns the first goal it encounters, which may be far deeper / more expensive than a shallower goal. Example: goal at depth 2 down the right branch is missed because DFS dives left to depth 50 first.

Uniform Cost Search (UCS) expands :: The node with the lowest cumulative path cost g(n); uses a min-priority queue keyed on g(n). Equivalent to Dijkstra's algorithm starting from the initial state.

When does UCS = BFS? :: When all step costs are equal — both expand nodes in identical order. (Because lowest g and shallowest depth coincide.)

Is UCS optimal? :: Yes — always finds the minimum-cost solution, provided every step cost ≥ ε > 0 (no zero-cost cycles).

Depth-Limited Search (DLS) :: DFS with a hard depth limit L; treats nodes at depth L as having no successors. Fails (returns no solution) if the shallowest goal is deeper than L.

IDS (Iterative Deepening Search) strategy :: Run DLS with increasing depth limits L=0, 1, 2, … until a solution is found. Combines DFS's linear space with BFS's completeness.

Why is IDS the preferred uninformed search? :: It gets BFS's guarantees (complete + optimal with equal costs) at DFS's space cost: O(b^d) time, O(bd) space. Example: for b=10, d=10 it uses ~100 nodes of memory vs BFS's 10^10.

IDS overhead vs BFS :: Repeated re-expansion adds only b/(b−1) factor (~11% for b=10). Acceptable price for linear space.

Bidirectional search time complexity :: O(b^(d/2)) — two simultaneous searches that meet in the middle. Example: b=10, d=10 → 2×10^5 nodes vs BFS's 10^10 (50,000× faster).

Bidirectional search requirements :: (1) goal state must be explicitly known, (2)

Bidirectional search time complexity :: O(b^(d/2)) — two simultaneous searches that meet in the middle. Example: b=10, d=10 → 2×10^5 nodes vs BFS's 10^10 (50,000× faster).

Bidirectional search requirements :: (1) goal state must be explicitly known, (2) actions must be reversible (predecessor function exists), (3) must detect when frontiers intersect.

Search algorithm with best space complexity :: IDS — O(bd) linear space, same as DFS but with BFS-style guarantees.

Complete search algorithms :: BFS, UCS, IDS, Bidirectional. **DFS and DLS are NOT complete.**

Optimal search algorithms :: BFS (equal costs only), UCS (any non-negative costs), IDS (equal costs), Bidirectional. DFS and Greedy are not optimal.

What is the frontier in search? :: The set of nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded — also called the "open list". The data structure used for the frontier (queue, stack, priority queue) is what distinguishes BFS, DFS, and UCS.

Why is DFS NOT complete in infinite spaces? :: With an infinite branch and no cycle check, DFS commits to one path forever — it never backtracks to try alternative branches even if a goal exists nearby. Adding a depth limit (DLS) or cycle detection fixes this.

anching factor is 2

What is Intelligence  Intelligence:  “the capacity to learn and solve problems” ( Websters dictionary)  in particular,  the ability to solve novel problems  the ability to act rationally  the ability to act like humans  Artificial Intelligence  build and understand intelligent entities or agents  2 main approaches: “engineering” versus “cognitive modeling”

What is Artificial Intelligence? (John McCarthy, Stanford University )  What is artificial intelligence? It is the science and engineering of making intelligent machines, especially intelligent computer programs . It is related to the similar task of using computers to understand human intelligence, but AI does not have to confine itself to methods that are biologically observable .  Yes, but what is intelligence? Intelligence is the computational part of the ability to achieve goals in the world . Varying kinds and degrees of intelligence occur in people, many animals and some machines .  Isn't there a solid definition of intelligence that doesn't depend on relating it to human intelligence? Not yet . The problem is that we cannot yet characterize in general what kinds of computational procedures we want to call intelligent . We understand some of the mechanisms of intelligence and not others . The Dartmouth Conference and the Name Artificial Intelligence

What’s involved in Intelligence?  Ability to interact with the real world  to perceive, understand, and act  e.g., speech recognition and understanding and synthesis  e.g., image understanding  e.g., ability to take actions, have an effect  Reasoning and Planning  modeling the external world, given input  solving new problems, planning, and making decisions  ability to deal with unexpected problems, uncertainties  Learning and Adaptation  we are continuously learning and adapting  our internal models are always being “updated”  e.g., a baby learning to categorize and recognize animals

Different Types of Artificial Intelligence 1. Modeling exactly how humans actually think 2. Modeling exactly how humans actually act 3. Modeling how ideal agents “should think” 4. Modeling how ideal agents “should act”  Modern AI focuses on the last definition  we will also focus on this “engineering” approach  success is judged by how well the agent performs

Acting humanly: Turing test  Turing (1950) "Computing machinery and intelligence“  "Can machines think?" → "Can machines behave intelligently?“  Operational test for intelligent behavior: the Imitation Game  Suggests major components required for AI: - knowledge representation - reasoning, - language/image understanding, - learning * Question: is it important that an intelligent system act like a human?

Thinking humanly  Cognitive Science approach  Try to get “inside” our minds  E.g., conduct experiments with people to try to “reverse - engineer” how we reason, learning, remember, predict  Problems  Humans don’t behave rationally  The reverse engineering is very hard to do  The brain’s hardware is very different to a computer program

Thinking rationally  Represent facts about the world via logic  Use logical inference as a basis for reasoning about these facts  Can be a very useful approach to AI  E.g., theorem - provers  Limitations  Does not account for an agent’s uncertainty about the world  E.g., difficult to couple to vision or speech systems  Has no way to represent goals, costs, etc (important aspects of real - world environments)

Acting rationally  Decision theory/Economics  Set of future states of the world  Set of possible actions an agent can take  Utility = gain to an agent for each action/state pair  An agent acts

sts, etc (important aspects of real - world environments)

Acting rationally  Decision theory/Economics  Set of future states of the world  Set of possible actions an agent can take  Utility = gain to an agent for each action/state pair  An agent acts rationally if it selects the action that maximizes its “utility”  Or expected utility if there is uncertainty  Emphasis is on autonomous agents that behave rationally (make the best predictions, take the best actions)  on average over time  within computational limitations (“bounded rationality”)

Turing’s Goal  Alan Turing, Computing Machinery and Intelligence, 1950:  Can machines think?  How could we tell? “I propose to consider the question, ‘Can machines think?’ This should begin with definitions of the meaning of the terms ‘machine’ and ‘think’. The definitions might be framed so as to reflect so far as possible the normal use of the words, but this attitude is dangerous. If the meaning of the words ‘machine’ and ‘think’ are to be found by examining how they are commonly used it is difficult to escape the conclusion that the meaning and the answer to the question, ‘Can machines think?’ is to be sought in a statistical survey such as a Gallup poll. But this is absurd. Instead of attempting such a definition I shall replace the question by another, which is closely related to it and is expressed in relatively unambiguous words.” — Alan Turing, Computing machinery and intelligence, 1950

Turing’s “Imitation Game” Interrogator B (a person) A (a machine)

Necessary versus Sufficient Conditions  Is ability to pass a Turing Test a necessary condition of intelligence ?  “May not machines carry out something which ought to be described as thinking but which is very different from what a man does? This objection is a very strong one, but at least we can say that if, nevertheless, a machine can be constructed to play the imitation game satisfactorily, we need not be troubled by this objection . ” — Turing, 1950  Is ability to pass a Turing Test a

s? This objection is a very strong one, but at least we can say that if, nevertheless, a machine can be constructed to play the imitation game satisfactorily, we need not be troubled by this objection . ” — Turing, 1950  Is ability to pass a Turing Test a sufficient condition of intelligence?

The Turing Syllogism  If an agent passes a Turing Test, then it produces a sensible sequence of verbal responses to a sequence of verbal stimuli .  If an agent produces a sensible sequence of verbal responses to a sequence of verbal stimuli, then it is intelligent .  Therefore, if an agent passes a Turing Test, then it is intelligent The Capacity Conception : If an agent has the capacity to produce a sensible sequence of verbal responses to a sequence of verbal stimuli, whatever they may be , then it is intelligent.

What is an Intelligent Agent  An agent is anything that can  perceive its environment through sensors , and  act upon that environment through actuators (or effectors )  Goal: Design rational agents that do a “good job” of acting in their environments  success determined based on some objective performance measure

Example: Vacuum Cleaner Agent  Percepts : location and contents, e.g., [ A, Dirty ]  Actions : Left, Right, Suck, NoOp

Intelligent Agent  Rational Agents  An agent should strive to "do the right thing", based on what it can perceive and the actions it can perform . The right action is the one that will cause the agent to be most successful .  Performance measure : An objective criterion for success of an agent's behavior .  E . g . , performance measure of a vacuum - cleaner agent could be amount of dirt cleaned up, amount of time taken, amount of electricity consumed, amount of noise generated, etc .  Definition of Rational Agent :  For each possible percept sequence, a rational agent should select an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure, given the

of time taken, amount of electricity consumed, amount of noise generated, etc .  Definition of Rational Agent :  For each possible percept sequence, a rational agent should select an action that is expected to maximize its performance measure, given the evidence provided by the percept sequence and whatever built - in knowledge the agent has .  Omniscience, learning, autonomy  Rationality is distinct from omniscience (all - knowing with infinite knowledge)  Choose action that maximizes expected value of perf . measure given percept to date  Agents can perform actions in order to modify future percepts so as to obtain useful information (information gathering, exploration)  An agent is autonomous if its behavior is determined by its own experience (with ability to learn and adapt)

Intelligent Agent  Rationality depends on  the performance measure that defines degree of success  the percept sequence - everything the agent has perceived so far  what the agent know about its environment  the actions that the agent can perform  Agent Function (percepts ==> actions)  Maps from percept histories to actions f : P * → A  The agent program runs on the physical architecture to produce the function f  agent = architecture + program Action := Function(Percept Sequence) If (Percept Sequence) then do Action  Example: A Simple Agent Function for Vacuum World If (current square is dirty) then suck Else move to adjacent square

Intelligent Agent  Limited Rationality  Optimal (i . e . best possible) rationality is NOT perfect success : limited sensors, actuators, and computing power may make this impossible  Theory of NP - completeness : some problems are likely impossible to solve quickly on ANY computer  Both natural and artificial intelligence are always limited  Degree of Rationality : the degree to which the agent’s internal "thinking" maximizes its performance measure, given  the available sensors  the available actuators  the available computing power  the available built - in knowledge

PEAS Analysis  To design a rational agent, we must specify the task environment  PEAS Analysis:  Specify Performance Measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors  Example: Consider the task of designing an automated taxi driver  Performance measure : Safe, fast, legal, comfortable trip, maximize profits  Environment : Roads, other traffic, pedestrians, customers  Actuators : Steering wheel, accelerator, brake, signal, horn  Sensors : Cameras, sonar, speedometer, GPS, odometer, engine sensors, keyboard

PEAS Analysis - Example  Agent: Medical diagnosis system  Performance measure : Healthy patient, minimize costs, lawsuits  Environment : Patient, hospital, staff  Actuators : Screen display (questions, tests, diagnoses, treatments, referrals)  Sensors : Keyboard (entry of symptoms, findings, patient's answers)  Agent: Part - picking robot  Performance measure : Percentage of parts in correct bins  Environment : Conveyor belt with parts, bins  Actuators : Jointed arm and hand  Sensors : Camera, joint angle sensors

PEAS Analysis - Example  Agent: Interactive English tutor  Performance measure: Maximize student's score on test  Environment: Set of students  Actuators: Screen display (exercises, suggestions, corrections)  Sensors: Keyboard

Types of Environment  Fully observable (vs . partially observable) :  An agent's sensors give it access to the complete

dors, shippers, . . .  Actuators: Display to user, follow URL, fill in form. . .  Sensors: Web pages (text, graphics, scripts. . .)

Types of Environment  Fully observable (vs . partially observable) :  An agent's sensors give it access to the complete state of the environment at each point in time .  Is everything an agent requires to choose its actions available to it via its sensors?  If so, the environment is fully accessible  If not, parts of the environment are inaccessible  Agent must make informed guesses about world

Types of Environment  Deterministic (vs . stochastic) :  The next state of the environment is completely determined by the current state and the action executed by the agent . (If the environment is deterministic except for the actions of other agents, then the environment is strategic ) .  Does the change in world state  Depend only on current state and agent’s action?  Non - deterministic environments  Have aspects beyond the control of the agent  Utility functions have to guess at changes in world

Types of Environment  Episodic (vs . sequential) :  The agent's experience is divided into atomic "episodes" (each episode consists of the agent perceiving and then performing a single action), and the choice of action in each episode depends only on the episode itself .  Is the choice of current action  Dependent on previous actions?  If not, then the environment is episodic  In non - episodic environments :  Agent has to plan ahead :  Current choice will affect future actions

Types of Environment  Static (vs . dynamic) :  The environment is unchanged while an agent is deliberating (the environment is semi - dynamic if the environment itself does not change with the passage of time but the agent's performance score does) .  Static environments don’t change  While the agent is deliberating over what to do  Dynamic environments do change  So agent should/could consult the world when choosing actions  Alternatively: anticipate the change during deliberation OR make

does) .  Static environments don’t change  While the agent is deliberating over what to do  Dynamic environments do change  So agent should/could consult the world when choosing actions  Alternatively: anticipate the change during deliberation OR make decision very fast

Types of Environment  Discrete (vs . continuous) :  A limited number of distinct, clearly defined percepts and actions .  It is continuous if there is a big range of values

Types of Environment  Single agent (vs . multi - agent) :  An agent operating by itself in an environment .  Multi Agent  There are many agents working together

Types of Environment  The environment type largely determines the agent design .  The real world is (of course) partially observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic, continuous, multi - agent

Agent Types  Simple ref lex agents  are based on condition - action rules and implemented with an appropriate production system. They are stateless devices which do not have memory of past world states.  Ref lex Agents with memory (Model - Based)  have internal state which is used to keep track of past states of the world.  Agents with goals  are agents which in addition to state information have a kind of goal information which describes desirable situations. Agents of this kind take future events into consideration.  Utility - based agents  base their decision on classic axiomatic utility - theory in order to act rationally. Note: All of these can be turned into “learning” agents

A Simple Reflex Agent  We can summarize part of the table by formulating commonly occurring patterns as condition - action rules :  Example : if car - in - front - brakes then initiate braking  Agent works by finding a rule whose condition matches the current situation  rule - based systems  But, this only works if the current percept is sufficient for making the correct decision function Simple - Reflex - Agent( percept ) returns action static: rules , a set of condition - action rules state  Interpret - Input( percept ) rule  Rule - Match( state, rules ) action  Rule - Action[ rule ] return action

Reflex Agent with Memory  Updating internal state requires two kinds of encoded knowledge  knowledge about how the world changes (independent of the agents’ actions)  knowledge about how the agents’ actions affect the world  But, knowledge of the internal state is not always enough  how to choose among alternative decision paths (e.g., where should the car go at an intersection)?  Requires knowledge of the goal to be achieved function Reflex - Agent - With - State(

e world  But, knowledge of the internal state is not always enough  how to choose among alternative decision paths (e.g., where should the car go at an intersection)?  Requires knowledge of the goal to be achieved function Reflex - Agent - With - State( percept ) returns action static: rules , a set of condition - action rules state , a description of the current world state  Update - State( state , percept ) rule  Rule - Match( state, rules ) action  Rule - Action[ rule ] state  Update - State( state , action ) return action

Agents with Explicit Goals  Reasoning about actions  reflex agents only act based on pre - computed knowledge (rules)  goal - based (planning) act by reasoning about which actions achieve the goal  less efficient, but more adaptive and f lexible

Agents with Explicit Goals  Knowing current state is not always enough.  State allows an agent to keep track of unseen parts of the world, but the agent must update state based on knowledge of changes in the world and of effects of own actions.  Goal = description of desired situation  Examples:  Decision to change lanes depends on a goal to go somewhere (and other factors);  Decision to put an item in shopping basket depends on a shopping list, map of store, knowledge of menu 

General Architecture for Goal - Based Agents  Simple agents do not have access to their own performance measure  In this case the designer will "hard wire" a goal for the agent, i.e. the designer will choose the goal and build it into the agent  Similarly, unintelligent agents cannot formulate their own problem  this formulation must be built - in also  The while loop above is the "execution phase" of this agent's behavior  Note that this architecture assumes that the execution phase does not require monitoring of the environment Input percept state  Update - State( state , percept ) goal  Formulate - Goal( state, perf - measure ) search - space  Formulate - Problem ( state, goal ) plan  Search( search - space , goal ) while (plan not empty) do

ire monitoring of the environment Input percept state  Update - State( state , percept ) goal  Formulate - Goal( state, perf - measure ) search - space  Formulate - Problem ( state, goal ) plan  Search( search - space , goal ) while (plan not empty) do action  Recommendation( plan , state ) plan  Remainder(plan, state) output action end

A Complete Utility - Based Agent  Utility Function  a mapping of states onto real numbers  allows rational decisions in two kinds of situations  evaluation of the tradeoffs among conf licting goals  evaluation of competing goals

Utility - Based Agents  Preferred world state has higher utility for agent = quality of being useful  Examples  quicker, safer, more reliable ways to get where going;  price comparison shopping  bidding on items in an auction  evaluating bids in an auction  Utility function: state ==> U(state) = measure of happiness  Search (goal - based) vs. games (utilities).

Shopping Agent Example  Navigating : Move around store ; avoid obstacles  Ref lex agent : store map precompiled .  Goal - based agent : create an internal map, reason explicitly about it, use signs and adapt to changes (e . g . , specials at the ends of aisles) .  Gathering : Find and put into cart groceries it wants, need to induce objects from percepts .  Ref lex agent : wander and grab items that look good .  Goal - based agent : shopping list .  Menu - planning : Generate shopping list, modify list if store is out of some item .  Goal - based agent : required ; what happens when a needed item is not there? Achieve the goal some other way . e . g . , no milk cartons : get canned milk or powdered milk .  Choosing among alternative brands  utility - based agent : trade off quality for price .

Learning Agents  Four main components:  Performance element: the agent function  Learning element: responsible for making improvements by observing performance  Critic: gives feedback to learning element by measuring agent’s performance  Problem generator: suggest other

our main components:  Performance element: the agent function  Learning element: responsible for making improvements by observing performance  Critic: gives feedback to learning element by measuring agent’s performance  Problem generator: suggest other possible courses of actions (exploration)

Search and Knowledge Representation  Goal - based and utility - based agents require representation of :  states within the environment  actions and effects (effect of an action is transition from the current state to another state)  goals  utilities  Problems can often be formulated as a search problem  to satisfy a goal, agent must find a sequence of actions (a path in the state - space graph) from the starting state to a goal state .  To do this efficiently, agents must have the ability to reason with their knowledge about the world and the problem domain  which path to follow (which action to choose from) next  how to determine if a goal state is reached OR how decide if a satisfactory state has been reached .

Intelligent Agent Summary  An agent perceives and acts in an environment . It has an architecture and is implemented by a program .  An ideal agent always chooses the action which maximizes its expected performance, given the percept sequence received so far .  An autonomous agent uses its own experience rather than built - in knowledge of the environment by the designer .  An agent program maps from a percept to an action and updates its internal state .  Reflex agents respond immediately to percepts .  Goal - based agents act in order to achieve their goal(s) .  Utility - based agents maximize their own utility function .

AI code of ethics  An AI code of ethics, also called an AI value platform, is a policy statement that formally defines the role of artificial intelligence as it applies to the continued development of the human race. The purpose of an AI code of ethics is to provide stakeholders with guidance when faced with an ethical decision

, is a policy statement that formally defines the role of artificial intelligence as it applies to the continued development of the human race. The purpose of an AI code of ethics is to provide stakeholders with guidance when faced with an ethical decision regarding the use of artificial intelligence.

Isaac Asimov  Isaac Asimov, the science fiction writer, foresaw the potential dangers of autonomous AI agents long before their development and created The Three Laws of Robotics as a means of limiting those risks . In Asimov's code of ethics :

Laws of Robotics  Law Zero : A robot may not injure humanity, or, through inaction, allow humanity to come to harm .  Law One : A robot may not injure a human being, or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm, unless this would violate a higher order law .  Law Two : A robot must obey orders given it by human beings, except where such orders would conflict with a higher order law .  Law Three : A robot must protect its own existence as long as such protection does not conflict with a higher order law .

AI Ethics AI researchers and developers to establish a set of guiding principles which are now referred to as the Asilomar AI Principles . This AI code of ethics mandates that :  The goal of AI research should be to create not undirected intelligence, but beneficial intelligence .  Investments in AI should be accompanied by funding for research on ensuring its beneficial use .  If an AI system causes harm, it should be possible to ascertain why .  Any involvement by an autonomous system in judicial decision - making should provide a satisfactory explanation auditable by a competent human authority .  There should be a constructive and healthy exchange between AI researchers and policy - makers .

AI Ethics  A culture of cooperation, trust, and transparency should be fostered among researchers and developers of AI.  Teams developing AI systems should actively cooperate to avoid corner - cutting on safety standards.  AI systems should be safe and

AI Ethics  A culture of cooperation, trust, and transparency should be fostered among researchers and developers of AI.  Teams developing AI systems should actively cooperate to avoid corner - cutting on safety standards.  AI systems should be safe and secure throughout their operational lifetime, and verifiably so where applicable and feasible.  Designers and builders of advanced AI systems are stakeholders in the moral implications of their use, misuse, and actions, with a responsibility and opportunity to shape those implications.  Highly autonomous AI systems should be designed so that their goals and behaviors can be assured to align with human values throughout their operation.

AI Ethics  AI systems should be designed and operated so as to be compatible with ideals of human dignity, rights, freedoms, and cultural diversity.  People should have the right to access, manage and control the data they generate, given AI systems’ power to analyze and utilize that data.  The application of AI to personal data must not unreasonably curtail people’s real or perceived liberty.  AI technologies should benefit and empower as many people as possible.

AI Ethics  The economic prosperity created by AI should be shared broadly, to benefit all of humanity.  Humans should choose how and whether to delegate decisions to AI systems, to accomplish human - chosen objectives.  The power conferred by control of highly advanced AI systems should respect and improve, rather than subvert, the social and civic processes on which the health of society depends.  Risks posed by AI systems, especially catastrophic or existential risks, must be subject to planning and mitigation efforts commensurate with their expected impact.  An arms race in lethal autonomous weapons should be avoided.

Problem Solving and Search - P3 INFORMED (HEURISTIC) SEARCH STRATEGIES R A N I L M . M O N TA R I L , M S E C E

Outline ▪ Greedy Best First Search ▪ A* Search ▪ Recursive Best First Search ▪ Heuristics ▪ Hill Climbing ▪ Simulated Annealing

Informed Search ▪ It is one that uses problem - specific knowledge beyond the definition of the problem itself — can find solutions more efficiently than can an uninformed strategy . ▪ Generally called Best First Search . It is an instance of the general TREE - SEARCH or GRAPH - SEARCH algorithm in which a node is selected for expansion based on an evaluation function, f(n) . The evaluation function is construed as a cost estimate, so the node with the lowest evaluation is expanded first . ▪ Most best - first algorithms include as a component of f a heuristic function , denoted h(n): h(n) = estimated cost of the cheapest path from the state at node n to a goal state.

Greedy Best First Search ▪ The Greedy Best First Search tries to expand the nodes to that is closest to the goal, on the grounds that this is likely to lead a solution quickly, thus it evaluates nodes by using heuristic function, f(n) = h(n) . ▪ It chooses the locally optimal choice at each stage with the hope of finding a global optimum . ▪ Greedy Choice : 3 + 7 + 11 = 21 ( Not Optimal) ▪ Actual Choice : 3 + 4 + 20 = 27 . 3 4 7 6 20 9 11

Greedy Best First Search

Properties of Greedy Search

Applications of Greedy Search 1. Activity Selection Problem 2. Huffman Coding 3. Job Sequencing Problem 4. Fractional Knapsack Problem 5. Prim’s minimum Spanning Tree

A* Search ▪ The most widely known form of best - first search. Admissible Heuristic ▪ The first condition of Optimality ▪ An admissible heuristic is one that never overestimates the cost to reach the goal . ▪ If n is the optimal solution reachable from n’, then g(n) ≥ g(n’) + h(n’) ▪ Because g(n) is the actual cost to reach n along the current path, and f(n)=g(n) + h(n), we have as an immediate consequence that f(n) never overestimates the true cost of a solution along the current path through n . ▪ Straight - line distance is admissible : can’t hope for anything better than a straight road to the goal ▪ Admissible heuristic means that A* is always optimistic

Consistency ▪ The second condition of optimality . Also known as monotonicity . ▪ A heuristic h(n) is consistent if, for every node n and every successor n of n generated by any action a, the estimated cost of reaching the goal from n’ is no greater than the

chunks.text:
nsistency ▪ The second condition of optimality . Also known as monotonicity . ▪ A heuristic h(n) is consistent if, for every node n and every successor n of n generated by any action a, the estimated cost of reaching the goal from n’ is no greater than the step cost of getting to plus the estimated cost of reaching the goal from n : h(n) ≤ c(n, a, n’ ) + h(n’) ▪ This is a form of the general triangle inequality , which stipulates that each side of a triangle cannot be longer than the sum of the other two sides . Here, the triangle is formed by n, n’, and the goal G n closest to n . For an admissible heuristic, the inequality makes perfect sense : if there were a route from n to G n via n’ that was cheaper than h(n), that would violate the property that h(n) is a lower bound on the cost to reach G n . ▪ It is fairly easy to show that every consistent heuristic is also admissible . Consistency is therefore a stricter requirement than admissibility, but one has to work quite hard to concoct heuristics that are admissible but not consistent

Optimality of A* ▪ A ∗ has the following properties : the tree - search version of A ∗ is optimal if h(n) is admissible, while the graph - search version is optimal if h(n) is consistent .

Recursive Best Search (RBFS) is a simple recursive algorithm that attempts to mimic the operation of standard best - first search, but using only linear space. Its structure is similar to that of a recursive depth - first search, but rather than continuing indefinitely down the current path, it uses the f limit variable to keep track of the f - value of the best alternative path available from any ancestor of the current node. If the current node exceeds this limit, the recursion unwinds back to the alternative path. As the recursion unwinds, RBFS replaces the f - value of each node along the path with a backed - up value — the best f - value of its children. In this way
